speaker: Yanbo Zhang
title: Vibe Research: Framework, Trust, and Collaboration
abstract: The emergence of Vibe Coding has significantly impacted the programming landscape by enhancing developer productivity and lowering entry barriers for those without formal coding experience. Similarly, the scientific community is witnessing the rise of Vibe Research, though the methodologies for conducting such research remain underdeveloped. A fundamental challenge in this paradigm is the issue of trust, specifically concerning the reliability of AI-generated results and the integrity of outputs produced by others using these technologies. This presentation proposes a preliminary framework and conceptual analysis to address these concerns. Furthermore, the implications of Vibe Research for scientific collaboration must be examined. While individual productivity is improved, significant obstacles to effective collaboration persist, including the individual-centric design of current technical tools and the barriers associated with procedural transparency. When researchers are uninformed regarding a collaborator's specific use of AI, inherent skepticism toward the technology often exacerbates a lack of interpersonal trust. Furthermore, Vibe Research may reduce the discussion of technical details among scientists, making teamwork even more difficult. This talk will detail specific insights and practical experiences aimed at addressing these technical and non-technical challenges within the evolving scientific research landscape.
bio: Yanbo Zhang completed his undergraduate studies in Condensed Matter Physics at the University of Science and Technology of China (USTC) and earned his PhD in Complex Systems from Arizona State University. His doctoral research focused on complex systems, complex networks, and machine learning, with a particular emphasis on Science for AI. This direction aimed to identify methods and analogies from established scientific disciplines to advance artificial intelligence. He is currently a postdoctoral researcher at Tufts University, where he continues to explore artificial intelligence and investigate various forms of intelligence.

speaker: Byungkyu Lee
title: Using Coding Agents as Research Tools: A Plan-Based Workflow for the Age of Generative AI
abstract: Automation in scientific research is rapidly advancing through generative AI agents, raising important questions about the role of researchers in this new landscape. In this talk, I propose a plan-based workflow for collaborating with AI coding agents and human collaborators alike. Such workflows can expand our ability to explore data, generate research ideas, and iterate on analyses, while also making it easier to verify, document, and evaluate what AI agents produce. Drawing on practical examples, this session will offer concrete strategies for integrating coding agents into research workflows as effective and accountable tools rather than autonomous substitutes for scholarly judgment.
bio: Byungkyu (BK) Lee is an Associate Professor of Sociology at New York University, and a co-Director of Networks in Context lab. He studies social cohesion and network dynamics, exploring their impacts on individual, community, and societal well-beings in the domains of health and politics. He is also interested in using large language models, surveys, and social media data to develop indicators of social cohesion and polarization, predict public opinion, and simulate AI agents for advancing social science research.
